# RFC-0003: Recipe And Runtime Execution Contract

> **Version:** 0.3.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** test

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0003:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0003c-summary"></a>

This RFC governs recipes and runtime execution: recipe composition over reusable definitions, resolution of every referenced fact before external effects, the long-running server and closed-loop runtime workflows including image-backed launches, single-role and multi-role serving topology, serve parallelism, and experimental proxy routing. [RFC-0001](../rfc/RFC-0001.md) holds the specification overview and the authority map.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0003:C-RECIPE-COMPOSITION] Recipe Composition (Normative) <a id="rfc-0003c-recipe-composition"></a>

A recipe MUST identify a reproducible inference eval and bench protocol within the workspace revision.

Workspace configuration MUST retain named model, serve-profile, source-set, environment, eval, bench, workload-suite, recipe, and image definitions as nine distinct configuration classes.

A recipe MAY reference workspace-local named model, serve-profile, source-set, environment, eval, bench, and workload-suite definitions. A workload-suite definition names the ordered Eval and Bench measurements a closed-loop recipe executes and MAY name one Eval as the suite's gate; a gated Bench is skipped when the gate does not succeed ([RFC-0004:C-MEASUREMENTS](../rfc/RFC-0004.md#rfc-0004c-measurements)). A recipe MUST NOT reference an image definition.

Every referenced definition MUST resolve within the selected workspace revision before the first external effect.

Every executable recipe MUST declare one or more ordered server cases.

The first declared case MUST be the recipe's default case.

When an operator omits case selection, both `serve start` and `recipe run` MUST select the default case.

Case declaration order MUST be preserved by parsing, resolution, dry-run output, and record evidence.

Recipe cases MUST be able to reuse common server and measurement definitions while varying only their declared case inputs.

An ordinary operator MUST be able to select a recipe and, when applicable, a non-default case without manually composing the recipe's referenced definitions.

A recipe MUST define bounded lifecycle and measurement composition. The public recipe contract MUST NOT require users to author arbitrary task nodes or a general workflow DAG.

**Rationale:** The nine configuration classes preserve the capability and reuse boundaries needed by real inference work. Recipe-first selection keeps those classes behind the collaboration unit, while ordered case deltas express common parameter sweeps without repeated server and measurement configuration. Images reference recipes for validation, never the reverse, so the reference graph stays acyclic.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0003:C-RESOLUTION] Resolution Dry Run And Overrides (Normative) <a id="rfc-0003c-resolution"></a>

Server resolution MUST combine the selected workspace revision, recipe and case references, local bindings, machine and tool observations required for validation, and explicit invocation-scoped server-setting overrides.

An invocation-scoped server-setting override MUST take precedence over the corresponding recipe-derived server setting.

A server-setting override MUST NOT add or remove recipe cases, Eval definitions, Bench definitions, measurement ordering, gate policy, cleanup policy, or local binding values.

Workspace serve configuration MUST be able to declare a framework-neutral readiness timeout independently of framework-specific server settings; a capture-armed launch waits unbounded instead ([RFC-0004:C-WORKLOAD-PROFILING](../rfc/RFC-0004.md#rfc-0004c-workload-profiling)).

An integration response MUST NOT replace the resolved readiness timeout.

Resolution MUST preserve the source of each effective behavior-affecting value, including the readiness timeout.

Manual Bench resolution MUST combine one named Bench definition from the discovered workspace root, one explicit running server record stored under that root, the immutable endpoint, model, and server-control capabilities preserved by that record, the Bench toolchain owned by the invoking Inferlab release, and explicit invocation-scoped Bench overrides.

A manual Bench MAY use a newer or dirty measurement workspace snapshot than the serving snapshot.

A manual Bench MUST reject a target record whose record schema or preserved server capabilities are incompatible with the invoking Inferlab release.

A manual Bench override MUST use `--set <path=value>`.

A manual Bench override MUST patch the selected typed Bench definition and be fully validated before case expansion.

A manual Bench override MUST NOT change the Bench identity or kind, target server record, endpoint, model, serving integration, recipe structure, or local bindings.

A Bench that requests prefix-cache reset MUST fail preflight when the target server record does not preserve a compatible reset capability.

Dry-run MUST perform the same reference resolution, local slot binding, compatibility checks, resource checks, toolchain checks, integration lowering, typed override validation, and determinable case planning required before real execution.

For a static Bench matrix, dry-run MUST report every exact expanded case in execution order.

For an adaptive Bench, dry-run MUST report the initial probes, target metric and threshold, refinement limit, minimum resolution when configured, and adaptive policy identity.

The adaptive policy MUST define a next-case and final-selection outcome for every valid observation state.

Dry-run MUST NOT launch a server, execute a measurement, acquire persistent server-operation ownership, or create a record that claims execution occurred.

Real execution MUST consume the validated resolved result produced by the same resolution path.

Each adaptive next case MUST be resolved from recorded prior observations according to the reported policy before that case starts.

Real execution MUST NOT independently reconstruct recipe, server, or measurement facts after validation.

**Rationale:** Explicit overrides provide the required local experimentation flexibility without turning persistent local bindings into a hidden behavior override layer. Manual Bench takes immutable serving facts from the record and mutable measurement intent from the current workspace, so collaborators can edit measurement definitions while retaining both snapshots. Static and adaptive previews expose all information knowable before results exist without pretending that result-dependent probes are predetermined.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0003:C-RUNTIME-WORKFLOWS] Runtime Workflows (Normative) <a id="rfc-0003c-runtime-workflows"></a>

Inferlab MUST support a long-running server workflow in which an operator starts one resolved recipe case, observes its status and logs, and explicitly stops it.

Inferlab MUST support a closed-loop recipe workflow in which Inferlab starts each selected recipe case, waits for readiness, executes the recipe measurements, and stops the server.

The long-running server and closed-loop recipe workflows MUST use the same recipe and case resolution, validation, integration lowering, launch, readiness, runtime-handle, and cleanup behavior.

A successfully started long-running server record MUST remain in a running state until an explicit stop or recovery action finalizes it.

Inferlab is an experiment harness, not a production service supervisor. Inferlab MUST NOT automatically restart experiment servers or claim availability guarantees.

During a live start or closed-loop command, Inferlab MUST attempt cleanup after handled launch, readiness, measurement, interruption, or cancellation failures.

Recovery of process ownership after the Inferlab process or host terminates before a runtime handle is durably recorded is outside this contract and MAY require operator cleanup. The resulting record MUST NOT claim verified cleanup.

A closed-loop recipe run MUST attempt server cleanup after success, measurement failure, interruption, or cancellation.

The public runtime CLI MUST provide `serve start`, `serve status`, `serve logs`, `serve stop`, `recipe run`, and `bench <bench-id> --serve <server-record-id>`.

`serve start` and `recipe run` MUST accept a recipe identifier and optional case selection.

`serve start` MUST resolve exactly one case. When its case selection is omitted, it MUST use the recipe's first declared case.

When `recipe run` case selection is omitted, it MUST use the recipe's first declared case.

`serve start` and `recipe run` MUST accept an optional runtime-image selection referencing an image build record. An image-backed launch substitutes the record's host-platform assembled image for the locally installed serving environment through the same containerized substitution image validation uses ([RFC-0007:C-IMAGE-BUILD](../rfc/RFC-0007.md#rfc-0007c-image-build)), and MUST otherwise reuse recipe and case resolution, validation, integration lowering, launch, readiness, measurement, evidence, and cleanup behavior unchanged, including dry-run.

Profiling capture is the exception to that reuse: a host-side profiler wrapping the container client observes no server process, so a capture would claim a profile it never took. Until this specification defines an in-container profiler contract, image-backed resolution MUST reject a launch whose server processes carry profiling capture targets.

Image-backed resolution MUST NOT require the locally installed serving environment: integration planning and rendering MUST execute against the selected image realization through its entrypoint contract, under the same one-shot JSON operation contract ([RFC-0006:C-INTEGRATIONS](../rfc/RFC-0006.md#rfc-0006c-integrations)), consuming the adapter packages baked into the image's locked closure. A failed or timed-out in-image integration invocation MUST NOT leave its container running. Image validation inside `image build` MAY keep local integration lowering, because its entry checks already prove the build host's local realization before assembly.

Local bindings MAY declare an image-adapter deadline and, for a host whose container runtime rejects device-less container creation, one workaround device for in-image integration lowering. Both declarations MUST be validated when bindings load, and the adapter container MUST request no device when none is declared.

Image-backed resolution MUST reject a selection before any external effect and before any integration invocation when the selected record's serving environment or source set differs from the recipe's, when the record holds no assembly for the launching host's platform, or when that assembly did not succeed. An image build record selection MUST reject a recipe case that does not resolve to single-host local placement, before network resolution or any remote-machine preflight consumes that placement; the rejection names the distribution boundary — a built image exists only in its builder's storage, and this specification defines no image distribution between machines.

A containerized launch MUST receive every resolver- and integration-set process environment variable explicitly. The substitution MUST NOT infer that set by comparing values against the ambient environment, which would drop an explicit setting whose value coincides with the host's.

The containerized substitution MUST lower the launching machine's declared container hardware facts ([RFC-0007:C-IMAGE-BUILD](../rfc/RFC-0007.md#rfc-0007c-image-build)) into every server container it launches — each declared host device, the unlimited pinned-memory ulimit, and each declared capability — identically for image-backed and external-image launches. The adapter container MUST NOT receive device or capability grants beyond its declared workaround device.

An image-backed server record MUST preserve the immutable image identity, the image build record reference, and the image's workspace revision alongside the invoking workspace revision, so drift between the revision that qualified the image and the revision that launches it stays observable.

Workspaces MAY declare external serving images — digest-pinned references to serving images this workspace did not build, each claiming the integration its serving stack answers. A declaration whose reference carries no immutable digest, whose integration identifier is invalid, or whose identifier collides MUST be rejected when the workspace loads.

`serve start` and `recipe run` MUST accept an external-image selection naming one declared external image; the selection and the image build record selection are mutually exclusive. External-image resolution MUST verify through a read-only probe that the referenced image is present in local builder storage, and MUST NOT pull; a missing image fails naming the exact pull command. A recipe whose serve profile selects a different integration than the declaration claims MUST be rejected. The profiling-capture and gate-ordering rules of image-backed launches apply unchanged.

An external-image selection MAY resolve to a multi-machine placement. Presence of the declared digest MUST be verified through read-only probes on the controller and on every launch machine — the controller needs the image even when it serves no process, because integration lowering executes there — and a missing image MUST fail naming the machine and the exact pull command. Inferlab MUST NOT distribute images between machines; distribution is the operator's pull per machine.

A remote containerized launch MUST NOT require the remote workspace realization: neither a materialized serving environment nor workspace-revision equality is checked, because the image replaces the serving environment; the remote workspace path is a launch directory only. The substitution MUST resolve machine-scoped launch facts against each launch machine — the container user identity from that machine's realization rather than controller filesystem metadata, and absent pass-through declarations reported against the launching machine's environment. The per-machine facts the launch consumed MUST enter the record's evidence. Declared pass-through values MUST flow into the container from that machine's launching environment — expanded at launch through shell references — and on a remote launch the value MUST NOT enter the plan, the recorded command, or the launch script text. A local launch composes the invoking environment into its recorded env map; that is this specification's standing unredacted-records posture, not a defect of the channel.

Containerized server processes launched over SSH MUST carry the same runtime-handle and cleanup semantics as remote host processes. A server container can outlive its docker client, and the kill escalation that guarantees termination for a host process group never reaches the containerized workload; every containerized server process MUST therefore carry a container handle — a resolver-assigned container name in the recorded launch command — and cleanup MUST confirm the container's removal on its launch machine, where an already-absent container is the confirmation. Cleanup MUST NOT claim verification while a known container's removal is unconfirmed. A launch failure after the container may already exist MUST attempt the same confirmed removal with the assigned name on its launch machine.

An external image substitutes for the locally installed serving environment through the same containerized substitution, launched through an explicit command override: nothing about the image's own entrypoint or environment projection is assumed. Because an external image carries no workspace-side packages, the workspace MUST commit an integration-lowering Pixi environment named `adapter` containing the workspace-side packages ([RFC-0006:C-INTEGRATIONS](../rfc/RFC-0006.md#rfc-0006c-integrations)) and no serving framework; external-image integration lowering MUST execute inside the external image from that environment's realized packages, so the adapter version the workspace pins is the one that lowers, and realizing the framework-free `adapter` environment does not reimpose the serving-environment burden the image removes. Resolution MUST observe and record the framework version present in the image — the only qualification-adjacent fact available; an image in which the claimed framework is not observable MUST be rejected.

An external-image launch is not qualified by this workspace, and its records MUST say so: a distinct realization state, no environment-check claim, and evidence carrying the declaration identifier, reference, digest, and observed framework version. Runtime JIT cache namespaces MUST key on the external image digest ([RFC-0002:C-RUNTIME-CACHE](../rfc/RFC-0002.md#rfc-0002c-runtime-cache)).

Manual Bench admission and execution MUST NOT distinguish an image-backed or external-image-backed managed server from one launched from the locally installed serving environment.

The `serve start`, `recipe run`, and `bench` commands MUST support dry-run mode.

A manual `bench` command MUST target an explicit managed server record and MUST NOT select an implicit latest server.

A manual `bench` command MUST reject a server record that is not in the running state.

A manual `bench` command MUST reject a server unless every process represented by its typed runtime handles is observed alive.

Manual Bench admission MUST NOT require a separate endpoint-readiness probe; endpoint request failure remains Bench failure evidence.

No more than one manual Bench execution MUST be active against one managed server at a time.

Acceptance of a manual Bench execution and acceptance of `serve stop` for the same server MUST be mutually exclusive.

Every handled manual Bench terminal outcome MUST finalize its Bench record.

Every handled timeout, interruption, or cancellation MUST terminate owned measurement processes.

Every handled manual Bench terminal outcome MUST release server-operation ownership.

Stale operation ownership MUST NOT permanently block a later Bench or stop operation.

Recovery MUST verify that no live owner remains.

Recovery MUST preserve the recovery outcome when an operation record exists.

This specification does not define separate serve-scoped Eval or Bench commands.

**Rationale:** The two server workflows differ only in who controls the server after readiness. Using recipe cases for both workflows gives collaborators one reproducible unit and prevents dry-run, manual operation, and automated qualification from developing different serving semantics. An explicit top-level Bench attachment makes a long-running experiment server useful without hiding the target or turning the serve command group into a measurement API. Per-server operation ownership prevents measurement and stop from racing while retaining experiment-oriented lifecycle guarantees. An image-backed launch reuses the containerized substitution image validation already proved, so a qualified image becomes the serving substrate for the same recipe cases rather than a parallel serving workflow; compatibility rejections happen before any integration invocation because every fact they need is already known, and the recorded image identity with both workspace revisions keeps qualification drift observable. Executing integration lowering against the image realization keeps one authority for framework facts — the serving stack that will actually run — and is what makes a launch possible on a machine that never installed the serving environment. The capture carve-out prices an unkeepable promise honestly: a rejected request costs seconds, while a profile that cannot observe the server would enter the evidence claiming it did. Declared external images put official releases, colleagues' builds, and older baselines on the same evidence plane as qualified runs — one recipe, one measurement pipeline, one record shape — while the records refuse to claim a qualification that never happened; digest pinning makes a committed baseline mean one artifact, and refusing to pull keeps the harness off the network, mirroring the production workflow's registry boundary. Lowering declared container hardware facts through the one substitution keeps a single authority for what a container may touch on a host: the same declaration serves image validation, image-backed launches, and external-image launches, and the adapter container — which runs no framework code — stays outside the grant. Multi-machine placements extend to external images and not to build records because the digest names an artifact any machine can pull while a built image has no distribution flow; per-machine presence probes keep the no-pull boundary while making the missing-image failure actionable, and machine-scoped launch facts keep the substitution honest on hosts whose users, environments, and filesystems differ from the controller's. Skipping the remote workspace-realization check for containerized launches is what the substitution promises: the image is the serving environment, so demanding a materialized remote Pixi environment would reimpose exactly the burden the image removes. The server-container handle exists because a container is a daemon-owned object, not a child of the launch shell: a rollback that kills the docker client leaves the engine container running while process-scoped cleanup claims verification, so cleanup must hold the container's own handle for its verification to mean anything.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0003:C-SERVE-TOPOLOGY] Serve Topology And Role Graph (Normative) <a id="rfc-0003c-serve-topology"></a>

A resolved server topology MUST contain one or more logical roles, the independently routable replicas within those roles, and the launched process ranks that realize those replicas.

Every launched framework process MUST belong to exactly one logical role and exactly one replica.

Each declared role replica count MUST be positive and MUST equal the number of resolved replicas for that role.

Replica identity MUST be unique within the resolved topology.

Each replica MUST be realized by one or more process ranks whose rank identities are unique and contiguous from zero within that replica.

Each replica MUST have exactly one rank-zero serving entry point; nonzero ranks MUST NOT be treated as independently routable replicas.

Resolved topology and execution evidence MUST distinguish logical-role identity, replica identity, process identity, and rank identity.

A single-role server spanning multiple machines MUST remain one logical serving role and one replica unless the shared configuration declares multiple replicas.

Inferlab MUST support a prefill/decode topology with distinct prefill and decode roles.

The vLLM integration MUST support Mooncake and NIXL as selectable KV-transfer mechanisms for the prefill/decode topology.

The vLLM integration MUST support independent positive replica counts for prefill and decode roles.

A router or proxy MUST target exactly the rank-zero serving entry point of each selected replica.

A router or proxy MUST NOT treat a non-serving headless rank as an independent backend.

A multi-role topology MUST identify the serving function of each role and every directed link required between roles before launch.

Role links MUST distinguish request routing from KV transfer, bootstrap, and side-channel communication when those semantics apply.

A serve profile or recipe case MAY supply role-specific settings, replica counts, and role-specific component-aware parallelism using the shape defined by [RFC-0003:C-SERVE-PARALLELISM](../rfc/RFC-0003.md#rfc-0003c-serve-parallelism).

For role-specific settings, replica counts, and parallelism, precedence MUST be serve profile, then recipe case, then invocation-scoped override.

Different roles MAY require different accelerator counts, replica counts, parallelism, ports, and placement and MAY be realized with different process-rank counts.

The selected framework integration MUST lower framework-specific topology settings into logical roles, per-replica total accelerator requirements, role links, primary and worker readiness requirements, profiler controls, and internal endpoint requirements.

The selected framework integration MUST NOT choose concrete process-rank counts, machines, GPU assignments, ports, or runtime storage during topology planning.

Local placement MAY divide a replica's total accelerator requirement into ordered process-rank GPU groups.

Each explicit process-rank GPU group MUST identify one machine and one or more GPUs, and the groups for a replica MUST collectively provide exactly that replica's required accelerator count.

Multiple process-rank GPU groups MAY use the same machine.

Concrete GPU assignments for concurrently launched processes on one machine MUST NOT overlap.

When local placement does not provide process-rank GPU groups for a replica, the control plane MUST realize that replica as one process rank on one machine using the full replica accelerator requirement.

The control plane MUST allocate concrete machines, GPUs, ports, and runtime storage before final integration rendering.

The selected framework integration MUST render exactly one process invocation for each concrete process allocation supplied by the control plane.

The control plane MUST validate role identities, replica cardinality, process-to-role and process-to-replica bindings, rank identities, link endpoints, resource requirements, launch dependencies, and endpoint ownership before the first launch.

A resolved topology MUST expose exactly one public workload endpoint regardless of its number of internal roles, replicas, or process ranks.

Single-role and multi-role topologies MUST use the same runtime-handle, startup rollback, readiness, status, logs, stop, recovery, and cleanup semantics.

Making role identities, replica identities, process-rank allocations, or role links required adapter request or response data MUST use a new adapter protocol version.

An adapter MUST NOT advertise an earlier protocol version while using the required role, replica, and process-rank shape.

**Rationale:** Logical roles describe inference responsibilities, replicas describe independently routable serving instances, and process ranks describe what Inferlab launches and can clean up. Shared configuration declares portable replica resource demand; local GPU groups decide whether that replica runs in one process, across machines, or in multiple ranks on one machine. Preserving those distinctions supports nP/mD pools and heterogeneous prefill/decode experiments without encoding machine topology into a reusable recipe.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0003:C-SERVE-PARALLELISM] Component-Aware Serve Parallelism (Normative) <a id="rfc-0003c-serve-parallelism"></a>

Serve profiles and recipe cases MAY declare framework-neutral component-aware parallelism.

The component-aware shape MUST expose the following optional positive-integer components: `outer.tensor_parallel_size`, `outer.pipeline_parallel_size`, `attention.tensor_parallel_size`, `attention.data_parallel_size`, `attention.context_parallel_size`, `experts.tensor_parallel_size`, `experts.data_parallel_size`, `experts.expert_parallel_size`, and `experts.dense_tensor_parallel_size`.

An operator MUST be able to override individual components without repeating unaffected components.

For each declared component, precedence MUST be serve profile, then recipe case, then invocation-scoped override.

The selected integration MUST either preserve every explicitly declared component in the server-level and accelerator-serving role effective shapes or reject the combination as unsupported during resolution before server launch.

The selected integration MUST fill every omitted component needed to return one complete positive-integer server-level shape and one complete positive-integer shape for each accelerator-serving role. An integration-added router or proxy role that does not execute model inference MUST instead have an empty effective parallelism shape and zero accelerator demand.

Dry-run output and execution records MUST preserve each declared shape, the source of each declared component, and each complete effective accelerator-serving shape.

Placement and GPU allocation MUST consume whole-replica accelerator requirements derived from the complete effective role shapes.

The framework-neutral configuration and protocol surfaces MUST NOT expose framework-native flags or framework-specific parallelism algebra.

Adding the component-aware shape as required adapter request or response data MUST use a new adapter protocol version. An adapter MUST NOT advertise an earlier protocol version while using the new required shape.

**Rationale:** Attention and MoE execution can use different parallel decompositions even when they share one outer deployment. A stable component vocabulary lets recipes compare those decompositions while the strict integration contract defined by [RFC-0006:C-INTEGRATIONS](../rfc/RFC-0006.md#rfc-0006c-integrations) preserves explicit intent, rejects unsupported combinations, and records the framework's complete effective result.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0003:C-EXPERIMENTAL-PROXY] Routing Backends And Public Endpoint (Normative) <a id="rfc-0003c-experimental-proxy"></a>

A topology that requires request routing across internal serving roles MUST expose its public workload endpoint through a managed router or proxy boundary.

Routing-backend selection MUST be a serve-profile deployment semantic rather than a required one-off command composition step.

The routing backend MUST be selected independently from the serving framework, topology, and KV-transfer mechanism.

A routing-backend selection MUST NOT imply compatibility with every framework, topology, or KV-transfer mechanism.

Inferlab MUST validate the selected routing backend against the resolved framework, topology, and KV-transfer mechanism before the first launch.

An unsupported routing-backend combination MUST fail resolution without launching any topology process.

Inferlab's built-in experimental proxy MUST be the default routing backend when a routed topology does not select another supported backend.

The built-in experimental proxy MUST support the vLLM prefill/decode topology over Mooncake and NIXL.

The vLLM integration MUST support selecting vLLM Router as an external routing backend for the vLLM prefill/decode topology over Mooncake and NIXL.

A framework-provided external router MUST be supplied by the selected workspace serving environment.

A framework-provided external router MUST be validated and lowered by the selected framework integration into an explicit logical role and whole-replica resource requirement.

Inferlab MUST NOT require a separate global tool catalog or environment authority to resolve a framework-provided external router.

An integration MUST NOT advertise a routing backend unless it can validate its settings, render its invocation, and declare its readiness and endpoint requirements.

The selected framework integration MUST declare the internal endpoint requirements and routing semantics required by the topology.

Inferlab MUST allocate the concrete internal endpoints and supply them when rendering the routed topology.

Inferlab MUST retain lifecycle ownership of every managed router or proxy regardless of which integration renders its invocation.

A managed router or proxy MUST have the same durable handle, command, environment, log, readiness, failure, and cleanup evidence as other launched topology processes.

A routed topology MUST NOT be reported ready until its public endpoint and every internal backend required by its selected routing policy are ready.

A handled partial-start failure MUST attempt rollback of every topology process already started, including the router or proxy when it has started.

Eval and Bench workloads MUST attach to the topology's public workload endpoint rather than directly to an internal role endpoint.

The built-in experimental proxy MAY use a static routing policy over the configured ready backends.

The built-in experimental proxy MUST NOT be required to provide automatic restart, degraded-backend recovery, backend eviction, minimum-ready capacity, or production availability guarantees.

A topology that exposes its service directly without request routing MUST NOT require a proxy.

Routing evidence MUST identify the selected backend, its effective routing policy, its internal targets, and its public endpoint. An Inferlab-owned backend MUST additionally record its implementation identity and version. An integration-rendered backend MUST additionally record the selected backend identity and integration adapter version.

**Rationale:** Routing is a replaceable deployment concern over one role graph. The built-in proxy keeps the default experiment path self-contained, while framework-provided routers can be selected without introducing another global provider or environment system. Compatibility validation prevents a selectable name from being mistaken for an implemented framework and transport combination, and common lifecycle ownership keeps all backends observable and cleanable through the same server workflow.

*Since: v0.1.0*
